name: Clayton Peak
short_description: BLM public rockhounding on the Wasatch Front near Salt Lake City. Search for quartz, feldspar, and mica crystals in alpine terrain with sweeping views.
description:
Clayton Peak sits on Utah BLM land in the northern Wasatch Mountains, offering accessible rockhounding in a high-elevation alpine setting. The peak and surrounding slopes are composed of granitic rock that hosts small to medium quartz crystals, feldspar specimens, and mica flakes - typical minerals found in the metamorphic and igneous complexes of the Wasatch Range.

The Wasatch Mountains formed during the Precambrian through Paleozoic periods and were uplifted during the Laramide Orogeny, exposing deep metamorphic and granitic rocks. Clayton Peak's geology reflects this long history of mountain building and erosion, creating excellent conditions for crystal hunters to find quality specimens in rock outcrops and talus slopes.

This location offers a unique combination of hiking and rockhounding. The approach involves moderate elevation gain through mixed conifer forest, with rocky terrain increasing as you climb toward the peak. Quartz and feldspar specimens are visible in cliff faces and scattered throughout rocky hillsides. The higher elevation provides cooler temperatures even in summer, making this an excellent escape from the hot desert floor.

Because this is public BLM land, you are welcome to collect for personal use without a permit. The 25 lb daily limit per FLPMA is typical for such areas. Bring proper tools, water, and sun protection - the alpine exposure offers little shade. The views across the Wasatch Front toward Salt Lake City make this a rewarding destination for both rockhounders and hikers.
history: Clayton Peak's granitic composition results from Precambrian to early Paleozoic magmatism followed by metamorphic processes during the formation of the Wasatch metamorphic complex. The range was uplifted during the Laramide Orogeny 80-55 million years ago, exposing these deep crustal rocks to erosion and creating the mineral-rich outcrops visible today.
tips: Focus your search on rock outcrops and talus slopes on the north and east-facing sides of the peak, where weathering has exposed fresh granitic material. Look for glassy, six-sided quartz crystals in the matrix of gray and pink feldspars - these are easiest to spot on lighter-colored granite faces. Bring a rock hammer and cold chisel to carefully extract specimens from friable granite; the alpine terrain makes large pocket excavation impractical. Scout the area after rain or snow melt when freshly exposed material is most visible.
terrain_notes: High-elevation alpine terrain at approximately 9,400 ft elevation. Steep slopes with exposed bedrock, loose shale fragments, and talus. Thin conifer forest near lower elevations opens to rocky alpine meadow. Walking distance from parking to prime collecting areas is 1-2 miles with 1,000+ ft elevation gain. Footing is rocky and uneven; sturdy hiking boots required. Weather changes rapidly; afternoon thunderstorms common in summer.
written_directions: From Salt Lake City, take I-80 east toward Park City for approximately 30 miles. Exit toward Wasatch County and follow signs to Clayton Peak Trailhead near Mirror Lake Highway. The trailhead parking area is accessed via Forest Service Road 58 (Mirror Lake Road). From the town of Salt Lake City, travel approximately 45 minutes to reach the parking area. High-clearance vehicle recommended for the final 2-3 miles of Forest Service Road 58, which can be rough and may have gates restricting winter access.
road_conditions: Forest Service Road 58 (Mirror Lake Road) is partially paved then transitions to gravel and dirt. Surface is uneven with washboard texture in places. High-clearance vehicle strongly recommended. Road may be gated seasonally (November - June) depending on snow conditions.
parking_notes: Small pullout parking area at Clayton Peak Trailhead. Capacity approximately 5-8 vehicles. No facilities. May be full during summer weekends.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation equipment. Take only loose material and small specimens that can be removed by hand tools without damaging the site. Pack out all waste and tools.
rules:
Personal use collection only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Remove only loose material or small hand-tool specimens
Pack out all waste
nearest_services: Park City, Utah - approximately 20 miles south - has gas, food, water, and supplies. Salt Lake City area - approximately 45 miles southwest - has all services.
accessibility_notes: Uneven rocky terrain with significant elevation gain. No wheelchair access. Sturdy hiking boots required for stable footing on talus and exposed granite. Steep slopes in places. Not suitable for visitors with limited mobility. The hike to prime collecting areas requires moderate fitness level.

faq.question: What gemstones can you find rockhounding at Clayton Peak in Utah?
faq.answer: Clayton Peak offers excellent collecting for quartz crystals, feldspar specimens, and mica flakes. These minerals are found throughout the granitic outcrops and talus slopes on the mountain. Quartz crystals range from small to medium size and are often clear or milky white. Feldspar specimens show pink and gray coloring typical of the Wasatch granite.

faq.question: Do you need a permit to collect rocks at Clayton Peak, Utah?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal use rockhounding at Clayton Peak. The site is on public BLM land managed under FLPMA (Federal Land Policy and Management Act). You can collect for free as long as you follow the 25 lb per day limit and personal use only rules.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding near Park City, Utah?
faq.answer: The best rockhounding season at Clayton Peak is June through September. Higher elevations experience snow from November through May, making access difficult. Summer provides the most reliable weather and accessible hiking conditions, though afternoon thunderstorms are common in July and August.

faq.question: How far is Clayton Peak from Salt Lake City and what roads lead there?
faq.answer: Clayton Peak is approximately 45 miles east of Salt Lake City. Take I-80 east toward Park City for about 30 miles, then follow signs to the Clayton Peak Trailhead near Mirror Lake Highway. The final approach uses Forest Service Road 58, which requires high-clearance vehicles and can be rough.

faq.question: Is Clayton Peak rockhounding site beginner-friendly and suitable for families?
faq.answer: Yes, Clayton Peak is beginner-friendly for rockhounding since quartz and feldspar crystals are visible and accessible without specialized skills. However, the hike involves 1,000+ ft elevation gain and rocky alpine terrain, making it best for families with kids age 8 and older who have hiking experience. The 1-2 mile approach combines hiking with rockhounding.

faq.question: What tools and supplies do you need for rockhounding at Clayton Peak?
faq.answer: Bring a rock hammer, cold chisel, and geofile for specimen collection. Pack 2-3 liters of water per person since the alpine terrain offers no water sources. Bring sun protection, a hat, and a light jacket for temperature changes. A daypack helps carry specimens. Sturdy hiking boots with good ankle support are essential for rocky terrain at 9,400 ft elevation.

meta_description: Free rockhounding for quartz, feldspar, and mica at Clayton Peak near Park City, Utah. No permit required on BLM land. Scenic alpine location.
